ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for raw prawns with mustard sauce as a prepared dish. FOODON_03301710 (shrimp food product) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW covers shrimps and prawns as a commodity category. No specific code exists for raw prawns with mustard sauce preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227144002 covers prawn as a food substance. No specific code for raw prawn with mustard sauce dish.

serving.description: 1 portion (approximately 6–8 medium prawns with mustard sauce)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving consists of approximately 150 g of fresh whole prawns accompanied by a small dish of mustard dipping sauce, equivalent to roughly 6–8 medium-sized prawns.

nutrient_source.title: Shrimp, raw (USDA FoodData Central, SR Legacy, NDB No. 15150)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are based on USDA SR Legacy raw shrimp (mixed species). The mustard dipping sauce contributes negligible macronutrient mass relative to the prawn portion and is accounted for by a modest sodium adjustment. Values represent the prawn component per 100 g edible portion. This is the best available approved-source match; no HPB Singapore Foodo entry exists for this specific Vietnamese-style raw prawn dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 (Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition)
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for raw prawns with mustard sauce. Prawns and shrimp are high-protein, very low-carbohydrate foods. GI is estimated at 5 based on analogous high-protein seafood entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2021 Atkinson et al. international GI tables, which consistently place protein-dominant seafood in the GI range of 0–10. Glycemic load per 150 g serving is 0.1 (carbohydrate_g 0.9 × GI 5 / 100 × 1.5 = 0.07, rounded to 0.1).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from prawns and mustard dipping sauce
hypertension.guidance: A 150 g serving of raw prawns with mustard sauce provides approximately 850 mg sodium, which represents a substantial portion of the 1500–2000 mg daily sodium target recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-sodium sauce are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and negligible glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 0.9 g carbohydrate per 100 g and a glycemic load of approximately 0.1 per serving, raw prawns with mustard sauce have a negligible impact on postprandial blood glucose and are suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes as part of a balanced meal plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary cholesterol and sodium, offset by low saturated fat and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Prawns are high in dietary cholesterol (approximately 189 mg per 100 g) and the mustard sauce adds sodium, which warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease; however, the low saturated fat content and presence of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids provide a partially offsetting cardioprotective benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tôm Sống Mù Tạt (Raw Prawns with Mustard Sauce) is a Vietnamese-origin seafood dish served at Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore, including Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant on Circular Road. The dish consists of fresh, whole raw prawns — typically tiger prawns or medium sea prawns — served chilled and accompanied by a pungent mustard-based dipping sauce, which may incorporate Japanese karashi mustard, soy sauce, and lime juice. The prawns are not cooked; they are presented in their natural state, similar in concept to sashimi-style seafood preparations. The texture is firm and slightly sweet, with a clean oceanic flavour, while the mustard sauce provides a sharp, spicy, and tangy contrast. Nutritionally, raw prawns are an excellent source of lean protein (approximately 20 g per 100 g), low in fat (1.7 g per 100 g), and virtually carbohydrate-free, making them highly suitable for low-carbohydrate and high-protein dietary patterns. They are rich in selenium, vitamin B12, phosphorus, and iodine. The primary clinical consideration is sodium, which is elevated due to both the natural sodium content of prawns and the mustard dipping sauce; individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size. The glycemic impact is negligible, making this dish appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Food safety is an important consideration given the raw preparation; the dish is intended for consumption at reputable establishments with verified cold-chain handling.

confidence_reason: The food name 'Tôm Sống Mù Tạt' is a Vietnamese dish served at a Vietnamese restaurant in Singapore. No exact entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN for this specific preparation. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy raw shrimp data, which is an approved secondary source and represents the dominant component of the dish. The mustard sauce contributes negligible macronutrient mass. GI is estimated by analogy from peer-reviewed GI tables for high-protein seafood. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact approved-source match exists for this specific dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are based on USDA SR Legacy raw shrimp (mixed species, NDB 15150) as the closest approved-source match. No HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry was found for this specific Vietnamese raw prawn mustard dish. 2. Sodium value (566 mg per 100 g) reflects the prawn component; actual dish sodium will be higher due to the mustard dipping sauce, estimated to add 100–200 mg per serving. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for raw shrimp. 4. sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for raw shrimp. 5. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a pure animal-protein seafood product with no plant fibre. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for raw shrimp (NDB 15150). 7. GI is estimated at 5 based on analogous high-protein seafood in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. 8. Food safety note: raw prawn consumption carries microbiological risk; this dish is intended for consumption at licensed food establishments with verified cold-chain handling. 9. The cuisine tag in the input is 'Singapore' but the dish is Vietnamese in origin, served at a Vietnamese restaurant in Singapore; cuisine has been corrected to 'Vietnamese' with region noted as 'Vietnam (served in Singapore)'. 10. Price SGD 25.00 per portion as recorded in GrabFood Singapore source data.
